perturbation.original_text: In the event that CIF requests that Dealer return any of its Confidential Information, Dealer agrees to return all of the Confidential Information delivered to it, and each copy or duplicate thereof, within thirty (30) days of the date of the request thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that CIF requests that Dealer return any of its Confidential Information, Dealer agrees to return all of the Confidential Information delivered to it, and each copy or duplicate thereof, within ninety (90) days of the date of the request thereof.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the return period to 90 days may contradict laws requiring quicker return of confidential information, depending on the specific type of information and applicable regulations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (PIPEDA)
perturbation.law_explanation: PIPEDA, Principle 4.9.4 states that 'an organization shall retain personal information only as long as necessary to fulfill the purposes for which it was collected.' Retaining information for 90 days after a request might be longer than 'necessary,' contradicting PIPEDA.

and (o) Â prescribing special procedures for giving individuals access under subsection 12(1) to personal informat
perturbation.justification: The original text requires the Dealer to return Confidential Information within 30 days, while the changed text extends this period to 90 days. The Law Explanation states that this change may contradict PIPEDA, Principle 4.9.4, which requires retaining personal information only as long as necessary. Although 'necessary' is subjective, the Perturbation Explanation suggests that retaining information for 90 days after a request may be longer than necessary, creating a potential contradiction. Scraped Law Snippet 1, Article 77 (b) allows the Governor in Council to prescribe the period of time for which any class of personal information is to be retained, which indirectly supports the idea that retention periods are legally regulated and that the change could be problematic if a shorter retention period is mandated elsewhere. The altered duration introduces an inconsistency with potential regulations, making the contract non-compliant and the change a legal contradiction under the Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction definition.

perturbation.original_text: The parties agree that the foregoing obligations of confidentiality and non- disclosure shall terminate five (5) years from the Effective Date of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties agree that the foregoing obligations of confidentiality and non- disclosure shall terminate one (1) year from the Effective Date of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality term to one year may be insufficient to protect trade secrets under applicable laws, potentially contradicting reasonable protection standards.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the Limitations Act sets a basic limitation period, a one-year confidentiality term might be deemed unreasonably short to protect proprietary information, potentially causing irreparable harm that wouldn't be actionable within a one-year window, conflicting with the intent to protect trade secrets which may have a longer lifespan.

perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the confidentiality term from five years to one year. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation state this may be insufficient to protect trade secrets, potentially causing irreparable harm not actionable within one year, conflicting with the intent to protect trade secrets. Given that 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' arises when 'time-sensitive obligations' in a contract do not align with legal requirements and the contract sets 'a deadline or requirement that violates federal or state law', the shortened confidentiality period can be seen as such a violation. Scraped Law Snippet 1, the Limitations Act, indicates that limitation periods apply despite agreements, suggesting that even if a contract specifies a shorter period, the law might provide a longer timeframe for action if the standard one year is insufficient.

perturbation.original_text: Any notice required or permitted u nder this Agreement shall be in writing and shall be delivered and deemed received as follows: (i) by personal delivery or by facsimile (with electronic confirmation) during the recipient’s normal business hours, in which case it shall be deemed received at the time of such delivery or facsimile as the case may be; (ii) by certified or registered mail, in which case it shall be deemed received on the date such return receipt is signed; or (iii) by prepaid nationally recognized overnight courier by next day delivery, in which case it shall be deemed received on the day following the date of deposit with such courier.
perturbation.changed_text: Any notice required or permitted under this Agreement shall be delivered and deemed received as follows: by standard mail, in which case it shall be deemed received three (3) days after the date of mailing, regardless of actual delivery date.
perturbation.explanation: Deeming notice received only 3 days after mailing, regardless of actual delivery, contradicts common law principles of reasonable notice, especially if the recipient is located far away or if postal service is unreliable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Common Law Principles of Contract Law - Reasonable Notice
perturbation.law_citation: Implied Terms of Good Faith and Reasonable Notice
perturbation.law_explanation: The changed text may be deemed unreasonable, as it sets a fixed receipt date without considering actual delivery, potentially violating the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing inherent in contract law and conflicting with common law principles requiring reasonable notice.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: good faith | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you good faith Good faith is a broad term that’s used to encompass honest dealing. Depending on the exact setting, good faith may require an honest belief or purpose, faithful performance of duties , observance of fair dealing standards, or an absence of fraudulent intent . A fiduciary relationship creates a duty of good faith between the agent and the principal . The breach of this duty of good faith can lead to liability . Failure to act in good faith is known as bad faith and is generally considered to be a level of culpability greater than negligence . [Last reviewed in January of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE business law contracts wex definitions business sectors legal education and practice Wex Toolbox

perturbation.justification: The modified text sets a fixed receipt date of three days after mailing, regardless of actual delivery. This directly contradicts the common law principles of reasonable notice. The Law Explanation states this may violate the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that good faith encompasses honest dealing and faithful performance of duties. By setting an arbitrary delivery date, the changed text doesn't ensure honest dealing or faithful performance, as actual delivery may take longer, especially if the recipient is far away. This undermines the reasonable notice required and creates a legal contradiction based on Inconsistencies.

perturbation.original_text: Dealer shall not assign this Agreement without the express written permission of CIF.
perturbation.changed_text: Dealer shall have the right to assign this Agreement to any competitor of CIF without obtaining prior written consent.
perturbation.explanation: Allowing assignment to a competitor without consent directly contradicts standard contract principles and could violate trade secret protection laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Common Law principles of Trade Secret Misappropriation
perturbation.law_citation: Principles of Unfair Competition and Trade Secret Law
perturbation.law_explanation: Allowing assignment of the agreement to a competitor without consent creates a high risk of trade secret misappropriation. This directly conflicts with the underlying principles aimed at preventing unfair competition and protecting trade secrets. Facilitating transfer to a competitor is unreasonable and goes against the nature of a confidentiality agreement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: unfair competition | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you unfair competition The law of unfair competition encompasses torts that cause economic harm to a business through deceptive or wrongful business practices. It is designed to protect both consumers and businesses from unethical conduct that disrupts fair market competition. The term "unfair competition" can be used both broadly, to describe a wide range of wrongful business practices, and narrowly, to refer specifically to deceptive practices involving consumer confusion. Unfair Competition (in the narrow/traditional sense) The narrow category of unfair competition specifically refers to practices that mislead or confuse consumers about the source, sponsorship, affiliation, or quality of goods or services. The central focus is on protecting the commercial identity or goodwill of businesses. Typical examples include trademark infringement , passing off, and false designation of origin. These are often enforced through trademark and unfair competition provisions under the Lanham Act , particularly 15 U.S.C. § 1125(a) . Unfair Trade Practices Unfair trade practices is a broader category of unfair competition that includes a wide array of unethical or deceptive conduct in the marketplace, even if it doesn’t involve consumer confusion about product origin. It focuses on preventing harmful practices directed at consumers or competitors more generally. Examples include false advertising , bait-and-switch tactics, misuse of trade secrets , breach of non-compete agreements, and fraudulent business schemes. In short, unfair competition deals primarily with consumer confusion about branding or origin, while unfair trade practices deal with broader misconduct that harms competitors or consumers, regardless of branding or misrepresentation. Unfair competition law does not address issues related to monopolization , price-fixing , or other market dominance concerns; those fall under the domain of antitrust law. The Lanham Act (15 U.S.C. § 1125) covers trademark infringement and false advertising at the federal level. The Federal Trade Commission (FTC) enforces regulations against deceptive trade practices, especially those that harm consumers, through its authority under the FTC Act . These rules are set out in Title 16 of the Code of Federal Regulations . Federal law may preempt conflicting state rules, especially where federal trademark or advertising standards apply. Some states have enacted statutes specifically addressing unfair trade practices. An example is the Uniform Deceptive Trade Practices Act (UDTPA) , which many states have adopted to provide clarity and consistency. [Last reviewed in June of 2025 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Keywords TRADEMARK INFRINGEMENT competition trademark Intellectual Property Law FEDERAL TRADE COMMISSION antitrust law Wex COMMERCE commercial activities consumer protection wex articles business law business organizations unfair competition business sectors commercial transactions Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts principles of unfair competition and trade secret protection. The original text prohibits assignment without consent, safeguarding against potential misuse of confidential information. The changed text explicitly allows assignment to a competitor without consent, creating a high risk of trade secret misappropriation, as highlighted in the Law Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines unfair competition as encompassing 'torts that cause economic harm to a business through deceptive or wrongful business practices' and includes 'misuse of trade secrets'. Allowing assignment to a competitor facilitates this misuse, directly conflicting with the law's intent to protect businesses from unethical conduct.
